content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 22, 2017

Quinn M. Barrow
Richards Watson Gershon
City Attorney
City of Manhattan Beach
355 South Grand Avenue, 40th Floor
Los Angeles CA 90071-3101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-178

Dear Mr. Barrow:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Manhattan Beach City Councilmember David Lesser regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does Councilmember Lesser have a disqualifying conflict of interest based on his residence that precludes him from participating in Manhattan Beach City Council decisions regarding Underground Utility Assessment Districts 4 and 15?

CONCLUSION

Based on the proximity of the project to the Councilmember's property and the effect on the value of his property, we conclude that there will be a financial effect on the value of his property and that financial which a reasonably prudent person would recognize as influencing the value of his property. Thus, under the standards in Regulation 18702.2, the effect is material and the councilmember would have a conflict of interest.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-178
Page No. 2

FACTS

Undergrounding: Utility undergrounding is the process of placing underground existing overhead utilities, including power, telephone, and cable TV wires and related facilities such as electrical transformers. The City does not own, operate, or maintain any overhead utilities in Manhattan Beach. Instead, these utilities are owned and operated by Southern California Edison (Edison), Frontier (formerly Verizon), and Spectrum (formerly Time Warner and Adelphia) (collectively, "Utility Owners").

The Manhattan Beach Municipal Code authorizes property owners to propose the formation of an Underground Utility Assessment District ("Assessment District") to provide funds to have the Utility Owners remove overhead utilities and place their respective utilities underground. The City Council, however, must vote to create Assessment Districts. Property owners in Manhattan Beach have proposed 16 different Assessment Districts in the City since 1985. According to City records, six Assessment Districts were completed, seven Districts were dissolved, and three inactive Districts were established by the City Council prior to Councilmember Lesser's election to Council in 2011.

The Councilmember Interest: The Councilmember is in his seventh year on the Council and currently serves as Mayor. His Mayoral term is scheduled to conclude in November 2017 and his term on Council is scheduled to conclude in March 2019. The Councilmember and his wife own² a residence on Anderson Street in a residential area commonly referred to as the "Hill Section." The Hill Section is within the boundaries of a former Assessment District dissolved in 2006. The Councilmember's Property is located in proximity to District 4, which was dissolved, and District 15, which is inactive. If either Assessment District 4 or 15 are ultimately approved by property owners within their respective Districts, the Councilmember's Property would not be subject to any assessments supporting the undergrounding of utilities on his street.

The front of the Councilmember's house faces west. Utility poles, lines and infrastructure adjacent to the Councilmember's Property to the west are within a former Assessment District that was dissolved in 2006. They are not subject to the Council discussion scheduled for September 2017 of Assessment Districts 4 and 15.

At a City Council meeting held on June 6, 2017, residents requested that the City revisit undergrounding. Residents from the areas designated as Assessment Districts 4 and 15 requested that the City begin the process to "reactivate" Districts 4 and 15. As further background on those districts, the requisite number of property owners signed and submitted a petition to initiate district formation for District 4, and the Council certified the petition in 2000. After validating the signatures, the City initiated district formation for District 4 by securing preliminary design plans from Edison. District 4 was subsequently dissolved by the City Council in 2005. It was replaced, in essence, by District 15, a smaller District completely within the boundaries of District 4. District 15 shares the same northern boundary as District 4 in the areas closest to the Councilmember's Property. The Council certified the petition for District 15 in 2007. After Edison completed its

_______________
² Title to the Councilmember's Property is held in the Councilmember and his wife's revocable trust, the David and Elisabeth Lesser Family Trust.

File No. A-17-178
Page No. 3

design plans for District 15, the remaining utilities did not perform their share of the design work,
and no other work has been done on District 15. District 15 has been inactive for eight years.

At a City Council meeting in September 2017, City staff is scheduled to request direction
from the City Council on whether, and under what circumstances, to facilitate formation of future
Assessment Districts. Specific to Districts 4 and 15, residents are currently canvassing residents in
both Districts to ascertain the level of support for undergrounding and hope to present such results
in time for City staff to present a report on the results of such canvassing. City staff will not be
seeking Council direction as to Districts 4 and 15 until after such time the surveys-have been
submitted to and validated by City staff.

If District 4 or 15 move forward, they would require construction one block south and one
block east of the Councilmember's Property, and two blocks south and west of the
Councilmember's Property, to place the existing overhead utility lines into conduits underground.

The actual process of undergrounding the utilities will likely produce temporary noise,
vibration, parking, and traffic impacts, but any such impacts would be expected to last no longer
than 4-6 months for the entire District, but only 2-3 weeks for each individual block.

The potential permanent impact of the projects to the Councilmember's Property would be
the removal of the overhead utility lines from secondary scenic views to the southwest. The primary
view from the Councilmember's Property is to the west toward the Pacific Ocean, which includes
utility poles, lines and infrastructure located outside the boundaries of District 4 and District 15.
There is a secondary view to the southwest toward the Palos Verdes Peninsula, which includes a
small number of above-ground utility poles in District 4, and, to a lesser extent, above-ground
utility poles in District 15.

On August 14, 2017, you provided the following additional information. "Manhattan Beach
staff provided the following approximate distances using GIS:

• Pole 2: 554 feet from Councilmember's property line
• Pole 3: 492 feet from Councilmember's property line
• Pole 4: 390 feet from Councilmember's property line"

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 sets forth the financial interests that form the basis
for a conflict of interest. The interest pertinent to your question is in Section 87102(b): "Any real
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 of more."

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of the decision; and an
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,

File No. A-17-178
Page No. 4

denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest.
Property is explicitly involved if it is affected as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) through (6).

"(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a general (except as
provided below) or specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed
boundaries of the plan;

"(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning (other than a zoning
decision applicable to all properties designated in that category), annexation or
de-annexation, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district, or other
local government subdivision, or other boundaries, other than elective district
boundaries as determined by the California Citizen's Redistricting Commission or
any other agency where the governmental decision is to determine boundaries for
elective purposes;

"(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that
apply to the parcel;

"(4) Authorizes the sale, purchase, or lease of the parcel;

"(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or
other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the
parcel or any variance that changes the permitted use of, or restrictions placed on,
that real property. For purposes of this paragraph, any financial effect resulting
from a governmental decision regarding permits or licenses issued to the official's
business entity when operating on the official's real property shall 2 be
conclusively analyzed under Regulation 18702.1, rather than this paragraph,
without any separate consideration for any material financial effects on the
official's real property as a result of the decision;

"(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an
interest will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from
improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly
situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise
receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision;

None of these provisions would apply to your facts.³ In all other cases, Regulation 18701(b)
provides that if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot be expected absent
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably
foreseeable.

³ Subdivision (a)(6) would not apply to the decisions in question since the Councilmember's property is not
within either district in question, despite incidentally benefiting from improvements to his view from his home.

File No. A-17-178
Page No. 5

With respect the remainder of the materiality standards, the ones that are pertinent to your
question are:

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial
interest;

"(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property....
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice
allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission
determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property; or

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the
market value of the official's property."

Generally transitory effects (in this case lasting only two to three weeks with respect to the
sites nearest the official's home) on traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, privacy, noise levels, or
air quality, and odors would not have any lasting effect on the market value of the real property.
More significant will be the permanent effects of the decisions on the Councilmember's view from
his property.

There are undeniable benefits to property values resulting from the undergrounding project.
These benefits are the basis for the imposition of the assessment on the residents within the
assessment district. While the Councilmember's residence is outside the assessment district, the
benefits with respect to the improvement in the view is not solely restricted to property within the
district. The Councilmember's property is within 500 feet of two poles to be undergrounded and
within 500 feet of the district boundary. The Councilmember also has a view of three poles to be
undergrounded from his property.

Based on the proximity of the project to the Councilmember's property and the effect on the
view from his property, we conclude that there will be a financial effect on the value of his property
and that financial which a reasonably prudent person would recognize as influencing the value of
his property. Thus, under the standards in Regulation 18702.2, the effect is material and the
Councilmember would have a conflict of interest.

File No. A-17-178
Page No. 6

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl
